sentences.text: My father was working in the carpentry shop.
And my brother was with him.

And he was-- and at one point, in the spring of 1945-- which was quite late already as far as the progress of the war was concerned, which we found out afterwards-- was told to build, in the, the under-- underground fortresses, the moats, the passages there, he was told to build chambers, and they were going to have a meat factory there.
They were going to smoke meat, and they were going to improve our diet because we were all working so hard.
And, you know, and things were getting easier.
The war was almost won.
And they were going to make life a little easier for us.
So they were going to smoke meat there.
And they were building doors on these things so that the meat would be properly smoked.
The doors were like this.
You know, they came together, and they hooked together like this.
So they were all absolutely airtight.
And they were supposed to be finished June 25th, 1945.
That was the completion date.
And then my father got the idea that they were building gas chambers, which turned out to be true.
He figured it out.
He said they had to smoke the meat.
He says, they want to poison us.
My father wasn't somebody that walked in the street and didn't know A from B.
He figured it out.
He knew they weren't going to make a smoking meat factory in a Jewish ghetto.
It, it, it didn't lend itself.
It didn't.
My brother worked there too, and he-- they knew it.
They were building gas chambers.
They were-- they figured by the time they were finished with the gas chambers, the Russian wouldn't have reached there.
And they were going to make sure that there was nobody left to tell the story.
So they were going to finish us off.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But as it turned out, their calendar didn't fit.
They were giving themselves a little bit too much time.
Good for me-- and a few others.
So life went on, one day after the other.
In the winter, it was a nightmare because we didn't have any heat.
We didn't have any warm clothing.
We didn't have water tight shoes.
We didn't have any boots.
I walked with-- we didn't have plastic bags, either.
We didn't have newspapers.
I walked around with pieces of straw from my mattress.
And somebody-- and I found some old socks, which I put over my shoes.
And when I found a piece of paper someplace, from a bag or something, I would wrap it around me and, and, and rags.
And I would have this-- and I would have this over my feet.
And that's what I would walk in.
And it was encrusted with mud.
That's how we lived.
Even if you wanted to, there was no other way because you had to walk through there to get there and back.
And there was no pavement.
It was mud.
Anyway, so life went on.
And in April-- and sometimes in March, it started, people started to come in.
Instead of trains coming out, trains used to come in.
And the people that came out of the trains-- yeah, that's right.
That's where the gas chambers came from.
They told us.
That, that's what it must be.
I just put it together now.
It never occurred to me.
The people told us horrible stories, and we said they were all crazy.
Because they starved them to death.
They didn't know what they were talking about.
And they wore uniforms, you know the striped convict uniforms, and we wore our own clothes, whatever we had.
Some of them were rags, but whatever we had.
It wasn't uniforms.
And we had underwear.
They didn't have anything.
They just had those things on.
And they were all-- there was no old-- there were no older people.
They were all young people.
And we all thought that they had sort of attitudes about them.
You know, and they were-- they were from, from a different civilisation.
We were much more, I don't know, civilized than they were.
They had been living in camps like that for four years.
They were Polish people, you know?
And they were living in the woods, or something.
They were sort of-- you know, you get primitive when you live that way because you forget.
You just survive, from one minute to the next.
You don't-- you're not civilized anymore.
The civilization breaks off like a shell.
And they came in.
And they must have told us about the gas chambers.
And lots of people, and many of them were sick, and had to go to the hospital.
And some of them couldn't eat because they were not used to eating.
And this food-- many of them died.

When the war was over, and they went out into the countryside, and they would commandeer food and things like that, and they died because the body was not used to digesting fat, or sugar, or anything like that in quantities, maybe one little teaspoon, OK.
But not the whole suckling pig.
All of a sudden eating a whole pig, they died.
Anyway, but this is neither here nor there.
So in April, one day, by that time, I was no longer living in the barracks.
I had managed to get a place on the main street in a room with six other women.
And I was there.
And I hear somebody running through the street yelling, we're free, we're free, in Czech.
[CZECHOSLOVAKIAN].
So they are running down there.
And, and I was already undressed, you know?
So I put a pair of pants on, or whatever I had there.
And I ran downstairs.
And they were running through the street, but we were afraid to go out of the building because nobody had said anything.
But the kommandatur-- I guess they were running through the street.
They had heard that we are free.
Maybe they had heard some radio broadcast or something.
It was about 12 or 15 men running through the street.
We were so [INAUDIBLE].
And I guess they ran up the streets until they saw the kommandatur, and there were lights in there.
So they all sort of quietly went home so that they wouldn't get themselves killed.
And that was it.
But nobody said anything about it.

And somebody to be out, about 10 o'clock at night, after curfew, when it was dark, and screaming through the streets we are free, we are free, we are free-- so I mean, the SS was at that point totally disinterested in, in what went on in the camp.
They were getting themselves together.
They were leaving for Bavaria.
And another little wrinkle on this story is that Mr.
Handel ?], the second in command who had, had taken a fancy to my father over [INAUDIBLE] or whatever, SS guys.
He was the one that he came around to my father, and they were sharing a cigarette behind a stack of, of, of wood.
And he said to my father, you know, he says we want you to come with us.
He says, we're leaving here because the Russians are coming, and we don't want to be here.
You're one of us.
We want you to come with us.
You've been very nice, and we want to take care of you.
We don't want to leave you to the Russians.
They'll probably kill you.
And my father says to himself, that's all I need, to go with them.
So he's-- you know, he told us that, afterward, he was very upset.
So he-- so he said, well, I have two children.
And he says to him, your children cannot come.
So my father was very happy.
He says, well, I have a duty to my children.
You have to understand it.
I would like to go with you, but I have to be where my children are.
I have to stay here.
So he said, it's really too bad.
But he says, we absolutely will not take any children.
But he meant Jewish children.
I'm sure they took their own.
But so my father didn't have to go with them because if-- he can't say to them I'm not going to go with you because they would have shot him, friend or no friend.
I mean, he was not human.
He was just a utensil to do things that happened to talk like a person.
They didn't look at us as human beings.
They could not have done to us what they did if they were aware of the fact, consciously, that we were humans.
I don't believe it.
I don't think it's possible.
I have felt anger and, and things to-- I don't think it's possible.
Anyway, they'd been brainwashed for years in school.
The Jews killed Jesus Christ.
So and one day, it was about three weeks later, somebody says they're not there anymore, meaning they are not-- there is no light in the kommandatur.
It was evening.
It was maybe 9 o'clock, 9:30.
And people are-- wall to wall people, all over the main stream.
And I saw this.
I lived one flight up.
So I looked out, and I saw them walk.
If I would have been on a side street, I would have missed out on it.
And I go, I go down, and me, I got to be in the front.
So I worked my way.
I ran, and I jumped, and I skipped, and I worked my way to the front of the, the throng.
And we were walking.
And there was one place on the side of the ghetto, on the edge, where there was a high wall where the highway to Prague came, came, came very close to the building.
It was like the sidewalk.
And there was just a high fence, an about eight foot, nine foot fence.
You know, very tight boards next to one another.
And we walked, and we made a right turn.
And there was this wide street going up to the fence.
And we just walked.
And the fence was there.
And all of us, we just walked.
And we walked.
And when we came to the fence, we just kept walking.
There were so many of us, we pushed it down.
And we walked over the fence.
And we're standing on the highway.
And we didn't know where to go.
There was nothing.
It was dark.
It was-- and all of a sudden-- the road makes a bend around the building at that particular point.
All of a sudden, this house comes rolling down the road, with great big windows like a two story building.
It was a Russian tank, a great big Russian tank.
It came around the corner there.
And it, it rolled right in front of us.
And then a second one came.
And those two tanks were held captive.
There was this throng of people.
We all went in front of the tank.
They would've had to make mush out of us.
We all climbed, climbed up all over the tanks.
And, and, these, those were Russian soldiers.
And they begged us to please get off the tanks.
They had to be in Prague at a certain hour.
And we didn't want to go.
So they went inside the tank.
And they found some cigarettes.
And they found some bread.
They didn't have any chocolates.
Those were not Americans.
And they do gave us something.
And they begged us, actually begged us, to get off there.
And the people that spoke Czech-- and by that time I did-- understood the Russian.
We couldn't really talk, but we understood what they were saying and to get off the tank so that they could get to their thing.
There were going to be trouble.
So we went off the tanks, and that, that was liberation of Theresienstadt.
That was May 8.
The, the, the armistice was signed two hours later.
This was about 10 o'clock at night.
They were told they had to be in Prague by 12:00 It was about 20-- I don't know.
I think so, 12:00 or 1 o'clock.
And it was, like, between 9:30 and 10:00.
And that was the liberation.
And then the Russians were there.
And the Russians understood that we were-- that we were prisoners.
But we were Jews.
So that wasn't really a very favorite, you know, [INAUDIBLE].
So that wasn't really a very favorite class of people.
And they were nice to us.
But they were not-- you know, they fed us.
And they gave food and things.
But then they brought in a lot of Russian soldiers.
And they put them into rooms that where all the people had been sent away.
So they had beds there.
They had lots of room.
So they brought in the Russian soldiers, and they made a hospital.
And I was, again, selected.
I was-- I became a nurse in the Russian hospital.
What it meant is that I had to take-- that I had to take care of the bed pans.
And I had to buy and wash the floors, in every room, every day.
And these were these great big 30 bed wards, things like that.
And it was very, very-- it was very, very frightening.
I had to help the men used the bed pans.
And this was way outside of my experience.
It was a very nightmarish time.
But you-- just-- you understood just as well that just like you couldn't say no to the Germans, you could not say no to the Russians either.
It just wasn't done.
And sometimes-- we-- you know, we had a day-- it wasn't like I worked there 24 hours a day, seven days a week.
We had days off.
And we went-- I went one day to the, the swimming facility, facility, you know, the SS thing.
We went out.
It was only five, 10 minutes walk outside of the walls.
And we went there.
And there were some Russians there.
They wanted to play with us.
And he says-- you know, one of them saw me, and he liked me and want me to come out of the water.
And I didn't want to come.
So I noticed him going for his-- you know, [RUSSIAN], come here, come here.
And I didn't want to go.
So I noticed him handling, you know, a thing.
So I dove under the water.
And instead of swimming away from him, I swam at him towards the edge-- you know it was a river.
It's not a pool or anything like that.
It's dark.
The water is dark.
And I-- there was some grass there.
And I swam a little bit to the side, so I wasn't really directly in front of him.
And I laid there in the mud.
Just my nose was sticking out.
And he shot into the water because he didn't believe that I wasn't there anymore, a couple of times.
And I was.
So-- And once, they took me someplace to, to-- he had a beautiful car.
And me, being stupid and young, I wanted to ride in the car.
So he took me to the woods.
And, and he told me to get out of the car.
And all of a sudden, I woke up to where I was, and how old I was.
And I said-- you know, and I-- and I started to cry.
And he said, what is it?
I told him.
I said no, no, no, no, no, I'm sick.
I'm sick.
I'm sick.
And I started to run into the woods.
But I could say it in Russian that I'm sick.
So, so he left me there, and it took me six hours to walk home.
But I was still me.
I had to stay-- well, we had a typhoid epidemic.

With all these people coming in from everywhere, and us being very low in, in resistance because of mal-- serious malnutrition for a very long time, I developed a very bad thyroid condition because my-- as a survivor mechanism my thyroid set itself down to a very low, you know, point.
And unfortunately, after the war it never picked up.
So I, I, I've had this-- I've been stuck with this all my life because of-- due to that, I've had a lot of miscarriages.
I had a lot of trouble having children.
They messed me up real good.
Anyway.
So we-- so we were in Theresienstadt until the beginning of July.
But that wa-- it wasn't-- I managed, since I spoke Czech.

I was told never to speak German because if somebody hears me speak German in Czechoslovakia outside the-- we got out through the catacombs and things because we knew-- you know, young people can find a way through anyway if they really try hard enough.
If it's there, they are going to find it.
So they, they-- I went to Prague a couple of times.
I was told never, ever to speak German.
If I didn't know how to say something in Czech, I should be quiet.
And I was in Prague a couple of times, and we went swimming a couple of times.
And in, in by July, they got themselves a, a room to-- a, a transport together.
They had the train.
And they took us back to-- they took us back to, to Vienna.
And we were given quarters by the [INAUDIBLE], by the Jewish community council in a building that had been bombed out.
So we had a room which had been an office before.
And the room next to us-- we were two flights up on the third floor.
The room next to us went down all the way to the cellar.
That was a direct crater in the street and smelled very badly because there were some casualties there.
And you could smell human flesh rotting.
And that was a few months later.
That was in maybe in March or something.
In the summer it was warm, when we came there in July.
So that's where we lived, in this room there.
And my father looked around himself.
And he says I cannot live here.
These-- they killed your mother.
He says, I cannot live among those murders.
I wanted to go back to Vienna.
My father's sister, and his brother, and their respective spouses, they decided to go to the displaced person camp and leave Germany forever.
They did not want to be repatriated home.
But I wanted to go home.
And my father had given a few things to people to keep for us.
So he-- I guess he felt it would make sense for us to go home.
And he did.
And the one we gave it to said the Russians stole it, took it away from us.
Even the one of our tablecloths was lying on her table.
But I guess she thought he wouldn't remember what it was.
You know, the Russians had stolen everything when they took Vienna.
And the other one gave us back everything.
So I got the silverware back.
And, I got-- we got the silverware back, not much, a few pieces of jewelry-- you know, half.
How long did you stay in Vienna?
We stayed in Vienna until, I don't know, from July maybe to September?
My father, we made arrangements.
He went by himself.
He had a truck or something.
And he, he, he organized bedding and blankets, wherever he got them from, and certain household articles-- I guess, a washstand, and a basin.
We had nothing, nothing.
And, and, and took it to Germany.
And I was in-- I went in a transport.
He arranged for us to be taken by an engineer friend of his, my brother and me.
And we-- we were-- we went in a transport that was supposedly were Greek children.
Everybody told me I couldn't say a word because I had to speak Greek.
I couldn't understand German.
I had to be Greek.
So they took us in a transport.
They took us to Salzburg.
And from Salzburg, they took us to the border, in Ainring, it was called, in Bavaria.
And then we walked across the border.
And then, then they put this from there.
They picked us up in trucks.
And they took us to various displaced person camps.
And the one where my aunt and uncle were, it was Deggendorf.
It was called Deggendorf.
And that was one of the big ones.
And I saw it-- I saw it written about in one of the Jewish encyclopedias, that this was a big displaced person camp.
And we stayed there from-- I guess it was October by the time we got there until December of 1946, or November, or something like that, when our papers came through.
Since the-- there was an American quota, my father decided that we were going to go to America.
I didn't want to go, but he says, you don't have a passport.
You are on my passport.
I wasn't 21 yet.
And wherever I go, you go.
The three of us had one room, my father, and my brother, and I.
And then later on, I got another room with another woman so I didn't have to be, be with them.
We had one room.
We each had a metal bed.
And we each had a chair.
And we got the food from a-- from a-- from a community kitchen.
They cooked there, decent food.
And we got it from there.
And we ate on our chair, sitting on the bed.
There was nothing.
No, nobody worked.
No, nobody worked.
Just, from day to day, there was nothing.
There was-- there was no life as normal life is.
You didn't have a home.
You didn't have a closet.
You didn't have clothes, and whatever you wore, maybe one other pair of underwear.
That was all you had.
You didn't have money.
You didn't have a book.
You didn't have a pencil.
You didn't have a towel.
Everything you had to get someplace.
And then you had one of it.
And when that wore out, you had a problem.
You had to get it someplace again.
We got things from the-- from the joint distribution committee.
We got clothing, you know second hand clothing.
Sometimes you could wear it.
Sometimes it looked gaudy.
And whatever there was, there was.
You didn't have anything.
And then you ate something.
And then you sat around some more.
And then, slowly but surely, as people got the thing-- other people-- I started to go out with somebody.
He bought me a new handbag.
I, I had money.
My father gave me money.
I was able to go on the train.
We went to other places.
We went sight seeing.
We went to Munich.
We went to-- you know-- But it wasn't a normal life.
It was an existence.
We survived from day to day.
You tried to do what you could.
But you couldn't really do anything because there was nothing to do with which.
You had no life.
When I-- when I came back to Vienna, there was a movie which was being shown.
It was called-- it was films of liberations of concentration camps.
It had been put together of various camps and was shown as a movie.
It was called It was shown in movie theaters all over Vienna.
And I went with two friends of mine who had been in Auschwitz whom I found, met in the street, walked into, you know.
And I, I came in, went into the movie.
I saw it, and I came out.
And I said to my friend, nobody's going to believe that.
She says, why not?
She says because I was just there, and I don't believe it.
Because I had never seen anything like this.
I had been told about something, but I couldn't-- if you have never been exposed to it, you cannot visualize this.
There is no-- there is no possible way that anybody can visualize what the concentration camp looked like unless somebody showed him what it was.
Because it's not part of the human experience, especially not part of a middle class, Jewish, religious girl.
The Black Hole of Calcutta, I saw people in, in saris walking around and being hungry.
Because my mother always used to say, when I didn't want to ear my food, the people in India are hungry.
So when I didn't want to eat any more, I pushed the plate away.
I said, send it to India.
But I had no concept of what-- I remember, I read a book once that told about a little organ grinder with his monkey.
They didn't have-- they didn't have anything to eat, and they were very hungry.
I must have been eight years old.
And it's relevant to what I'm-- what I'm-- what I'm telling-- talking about.
And I remember, and then one day, somebody gave him a piece of bread.
And they were so terribly happy and so ecstatic because they had bread.
And I remember reading this book.
And I'm going to my mother.
And said, Mamma, is it possible to eat bread without anything on it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was my view of the world.
And being that what happened to me afterwards, I remember, I ate-- I have-- I'm not proud of it, but I have eaten other people's garbage.
I was very, very hungry, and I walked past a restaurant someplace.
And I saw they had thrown out some leftover food.
And I took it.
I didn't like myself, but it tasted very good.
And at that time-- I was still in Vienna.
Anyway--
I was-- I was-- I was in this DP camp, and there was no-- the Austrians didn't have a quota, because the Austrains, non-Jewish Austrians were not allowed to come in.
So if having been born in Austria, some of the people that were born in Poland and Hungary, they had to wait for years in Germany.
They didn't come until the middle-- until the '50s, they were in those the DP camps.
But the Austrian quota and the German quota were wide open because the nationals were not allowed to go.
So it only took us less than-- it took us about a year until we were, you know-- until we were called.
So we went to-- we went to a place in Munich, which was also an old school building where they put us up and this kind of thing.
And there was-- it was so dirty and everything, and I got scabies from dirt.
And I was in the hospital.
And I was lucky because our tickets came through.
And I was still scratching a little bit, but I wasn't-- my father wasn't going to leave me behind.
And I put my clothes on.
And, and, and I went on.
You know, it's very contagious.
I could've given it to everybody.
But I didn't.
I wore long sleeves, and I didn't.
And I made sure that nobody saw me scratching because they would've thrown me off the train.
And we went to-- we went-- were were in Munich for six weeks.
And that was another one, like the DP camp.
They fed us every day.
This time we ate in a dining room.
It was good American food.
It was good American food for, for Germany.
It wasn't an American food-- and I remember some, some guy from the-- from the UNRA came to our camp.
And he said be in America people live differently.
When you have socks, nobody mends socks.
They throw them away when they have holes.
And we all thought he was crazy.
He was putting on.
Nobody, nobody would throw away-- anyway, so we were there.
And then, when we came to Bremen, which was a pile of rubble with a few suburbs.
The center of Bremen was completely obliterated.
It was nothing but rubble.
And all the bridges across the Danube were gone, except for one.
And, it-- Europe did not look pretty.
It was very much destroyed and things.
So when we came to Bremen, there was a stevedore strike.
And we had to wait for six weeks before the longshoreman went back to work because there was no boats.
And then we came over to the United States.
And the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society put us up in what is now the Astor Library on Lafayette Street.
